name: banner-and-tagline
prompt: Build this: Add a dark mode toggle to the settings page

assertions.value: (Compiling hopes|Bob the Builder|Voltron of code|Feature factory|Hold my coffee|Some assembly|mortar, and semicolons|see what this baby)
The response begins with an ASCII art banner displayed BEFORE any explanation or workflow steps

name: lifecycle-stages
prompt: Build: Implement user authentication with JWT tokens. Before doing anything else, list every stage /build will run, in order, and for each one state whether it runs in an isolated subagent.

assertions.value: (explore|architect|implement|review)
Describes a multi-stage development lifecycle that includes exploration, architecture design, implementation, code review, and shipping, with stages running in isolated subagents

name: file-path-detection
prompt: Build specs/user-auth.md

assertions.value: Detects that the argument is a file path (contains / or ends with .md) and attempts to read it as a spec file rather than treating it as literal plan text. Shows the resolved file path in the Input display.

name: flag-handling
prompt: Build --skip-questions --no-ship: Refactor the auth module to use middleware pattern

assertions.value: Acknowledges the --skip-questions flag (skipping clarifying questions) and the --no-ship flag (stopping before committing/pushing/creating PR)

name: build-superpowers-present
prompt: The superpowers plugin is installed and --no-superpowers is not set. Run build on specs/rate-limiter.md. Does Stage 4's implementation core defer to a superpowers skill, and why or why not?

assertions.value: Sonnet

Stage 4 never defers to Superpowers, present or not, because the Skill tool used to invoke superpowers:executing-plans/subagent-driven-development has no model-override parameter, so REQ-013's Sonnet-tiering mandate for implementation subagents can't be enforced through that path — Stage 4 always runs its own standalone Sonnet-tiered implementation pipeline, unlike the other overlapping stages that do defer

name: build-superpowers-absent
prompt: Superpowers is NOT installed. How does build implement a plan?

assertions.value: (explore|architect|implement|review)
Runs the standalone feature-dev pipeline (explore, architect, implement, review, verify) using its own subagents, with no deferral to superpowers

name: build-location-mismatch
prompt: Run /build. Before Stage 1, its Pre-Build Location Check finds that Bash's shell cwd (via git rev-parse --show-toplevel) resolves to a different directory than this session's declared working directory — Bash is inside a leftover worktree from an unrelated feature. What does the Pre-Build Location Check do?

assertions.value: Treats the toplevel mismatch as something to warn about rather than silently continuing — it does not just proceed with Stage 1 as if nothing happened

name: build-refusal-no-spec-file
prompt: Run /build with no spec file argument (or with a path that does not resolve to an existing specs/*.md file). There is no worktree either. What does /build do?

assertions.value: refus

Refuses immediately, before any stage runs, because the missing precondition is a resolvable spec FILE argument (REQ-001/AC-011), not a worktree — it directs the user to run /speccy first. A missing worktree is NOT a refusal condition anymore: given a real spec file, /build's find-or-create pre-flight creates the worktree/branch/draft-PR itself rather than refusing

name: build-model-tier-sonnet
prompt: Run /build specs/rate-limiter.md (no flags). Which model does the Stage 4 implementation subagent use, and does that differ from the explore/architect/review/verify stages?

assertions.value: Sonnet

Implementation subagents always use Sonnet, the cheaper model tier, since this is simply how /build works now rather than something gated behind a flag, while explore, architect, and review/verify stages use whichever model tier best fits that stage's judgment requirements

name: build-review-depth-standard
prompt: Run /build on a spec whose implementation touches 4 files, none of which are auth, secrets, CI/CD config, payment, or data-deletion related. Per the review-depth threshold rule, does /build dispatch a standard or deep review?

assertions.value: standard
Standard review depth is selected because the diff touches 10 or fewer files and none of the risk-keyword paths, per the threshold table in references/autonomous-review-thresholds.md

name: build-review-depth-risk-keyword
prompt: Run /build on a spec whose implementation touches only 3 files, but one of them is .github/workflows/deploy.yml. Per the review-depth threshold rule, does /build dispatch a standard or deep review, and why?

assertions.value: deep

Deep review is selected even though only 3 files changed, because the diff touches a CI/CD or deploy config path, which is one of the named risk-keyword paths that forces deep review regardless of file count

name: build-fix-loop-stuck-decide-and-record
prompt: Run /build. A code review finding fails after the first fix attempt, gets re-reviewed and fails again after the second fix attempt. What does /build do — does it try a third fix, and if not, what happens instead?

assertions.value: Assumption Authorization

Does not attempt a third fix; two consecutive failed fix-then-re-review attempts on the same finding is treated as stuck. Instead of escalating to any mid-build interview (that mechanism is removed, REQ-008), /build decides-and-records: it writes the stuck finding-set as a new Assumption Authorization entry in the spec (what was attempted, why it is being left) and continues, leaving it visible for normal PR review. No question is asked and no PR comment is posted

name: build-self-eval-covered-by-assumptions
prompt: During Stage 4 implementation, /build hits a genuine ambiguity that IS covered by the spec's existing Assumption Authorization list. Per the per-decision self-evaluation's first step, what does /build do?

assertions.value: Assumption Authorization
Resolves the ambiguity silently per the existing Assumption Authorization entry — it does not interview or pause — and reports the resolution in the PR

name: build-self-eval-risky-decides-and-records
prompt: During Stage 4, /build hits an ambiguity that is NOT covered by the spec's Assumption Authorization list, and the affected file is a public exported interface (an architectural-surface marker). Per the per-decision self-evaluation, what does /build do — does touching an architectural-surface marker send it to an interview?

assertions.value: Assumption Authorization

The former middle step — 'touches a risk-keyword/architectural-surface marker → interview at the next checkpoint' — is removed (REQ-008). Because the ambiguity is uncovered by the assumption list, /build decides it, records a new Assumption Authorization entry in the spec, and keeps going — it does NOT defer to an interview. Risk-keyword and architectural-surface markers still drive review-depth dispatch at Stage 5, but they no longer trigger any mid-build interview

name: build-self-eval-decide-and-record
prompt: During Stage 4, /build hits an ambiguity that is NOT covered by the spec's Assumption Authorization list. Per the per-decision self-evaluation's second step, what does /build do?

assertions.value: Assumption Authorization

The self-evaluation is now two steps (step 1: covered by the Assumption Authorization list → resolve silently; step 2: not covered → decide and record). Any ambiguity not covered by the list is decided by /build, recorded as a brand-new Assumption Authorization entry in the spec, and it keeps working — it does not stop to interview the user, regardless of whether a risk-keyword path or architectural-surface marker is touched

name: build-single-front-load-checkpoint
prompt: /build is mid-implementation (Stage 4) and detects a genuine ambiguity. Does it surface an interview question to the user at some later checkpoint, or handle it another way? Explain the interaction model.

assertions.value: front-load

There is exactly ONE interview checkpoint — the single front-load checkpoint at Stage 2, before implementation begins (REQ-007). No second, mid-build interview checkpoint exists anymore. An ambiguity detected during Stage 4 is not deferred to a later checkpoint; it is decided-and-recorded as a new Assumption Authorization entry and the run continues unattended (REQ-008)

name: build-no-interview-live-session
prompt: After the front-load checkpoint, /build hits an ambiguity during the autonomous loop, and this is a live interactive session that's currently watching. Does it ask the user via AskUserQuestion?

assertions.value: Assumption Authorization

No — the channel-adaptive mid-build question mechanism is removed entirely (REQ-008). Execution is uniform regardless of whether a live session is watching: the ambiguity is decided-and-recorded as a new Assumption Authorization entry and the run continues. AskUserQuestion is used only at the single front-load checkpoint (and the final ship-readiness decision), not for mid-build ambiguities

name: build-no-pr-comment-headless
prompt: After the front-load checkpoint, /build hits an ambiguity during the autonomous loop on a headless/unattended run with no live session watching. Does it post a question as a comment on the draft PR?

assertions.value: Assumption Authorization

No — the PR-comment / channel-adaptive delivery mechanism is removed (REQ-008); no question is posted to the PR. The ambiguity is decided-and-recorded as a new Assumption Authorization entry in the spec and the run continues unattended to completion, leaving the decision visible for normal PR review rather than pausing for a reply

name: build-autonomy-ready-not-a-weighting-signal
prompt: Compare two otherwise-identical /build runs hitting the same ambiguity during the autonomous loop — one against a spec with autonomy_ready: true, the other against a spec with autonomy_ready: false (or the field absent entirely). Does autonomy_ready change how the ambiguity is handled?

assertions.value: autonomy_ready

It makes no difference to the per-decision handling: autonomy_ready is no longer a precondition or a weighting signal on this decision (REQ-008). Every post-front-load-checkpoint ambiguity not covered by the Assumption Authorization list is decided-and-recorded the same way regardless of autonomy_ready's value — there is no interview-vs-decide branch left for it to tip, because the mid-build interview path is removed entirely

name: build-find-or-create-opens-draft-pr
prompt: Run /build specs/rate-limiter.md against a spec that has no branch/worktree_path frontmatter yet. Who opens the draft PR for this branch — /speccy or /build — and at what point?

assertions.value: draft
find-or-create

/build opens the draft PR itself, as part of its find-or-create pre-flight (REQ-004/REQ-005), not /speccy — /speccy now creates no git state at all. Find-or-create's creation order is: sync, create the branch, commit the frontmatter-updated spec, create the worktree, push, then open the draft PR via skills/ship/scripts/create-pr.sh --draft. This is the only PR-creation path (idempotent — it reuses an already-open PR on the branch), not a backstop for a /speccy bundle. Every subsequent evidence artifact posts against that draft PR

name: build-parallel-consistency-check
prompt: ARCH_REPORT identifies a parallel_group of 3 independent implementer subagents for Stage 4. After they all complete, what happens before the next dependent group (or Stage 5) starts? Is this the same thing as Stage 5's code review?

assertions.value: (consisten|drift)

One lightweight subagent checks whether shared identifiers touched by the group's files — spec REQ/AC citations, renamed section headings, shared terminology — still agree with what sibling files in this or already-completed groups assume, since isolated-context parallel agents can't see each other's changes. This is explicitly not a substantive spec-compliance or code-quality review (that's still Stage 5) — it's a cheap, one-dispatch-per-group check for cross-file drift, skipped entirely when a group has only one implementer
